---
title: "Limitations"
---

# Limitations and Future Work

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary
This section is an honest assessment of what the current system cannot do
and where it may fail. Reading this section before deploying or drawing
conclusions from this work is strongly recommended.
:::

---

## Synthetic Data Limitation {#sec-limits-synthetic}

::: {.callout-warning}
## Most Important Limitation

All development, training, and evaluation in this project uses MedSynth —
a synthetic dataset generated by GPT-4o. The system has **never been tested
on real clinical notes**. Performance figures in this document cannot be
assumed to hold in a production environment without validation on real data.
:::

The synthetic-to-real generalisation gap is a fundamental concern for any
model trained on generated data. Known differences between MedSynth and
real clinical notes include:

- **Uniform code distribution.** MedSynth has exactly 5 notes per code.
  Real clinical data has highly skewed distributions where a handful of
  common codes (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, low back pain) dominate
  the workload and rare codes may appear once per year.

- **Writing style.** GPT-4o-generated notes are well-structured, complete,
  and consistent. Real clinical notes contain abbreviations, typos,
  copy-paste artefacts, incomplete sections, and institution-specific
  terminology.

- **Multi-diagnosis notes.** Each MedSynth note is associated with exactly
  one ICD-10 code. Real clinical encounters routinely document multiple
  conditions in a single note — multi-label prediction is required for
  real-world use.

- **Temporal context.** Real notes reference prior encounters, ongoing
  conditions, and evolving treatment plans. MedSynth notes are isolated.

**Planned mitigation:** Validation on MIMIC-IV-Note (331,794 deidentified
discharge summaries from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center) is planned
but currently blocked pending PhysioNet data access approval. This validation
will quantify the synthetic-to-real gap and identify which chapters and code
types are most affected.

---

## Z-Chapter Performance {#sec-limits-z}

The Z-chapter (administrative contacts, 263 codes, 62.1% accuracy) remains
the primary performance gap. Z-codes cover:

- Screening encounters (`Z12.*` — screening for specific conditions)
- Preventive care visits (`Z23.*` — vaccination encounters)
- Follow-up care (`Z09.*` — follow-up after treatment)
- Administrative factors (`Z59.*` — housing instability, `Z60.*` — social isolation)

The clinical notes for these encounters are structurally similar and often
use nearly identical language regardless of the specific Z-code being
assigned. Standard cross-entropy training is insufficient to distinguish
between codes whose training examples are lexically nearly identical.

**Planned mitigation:** Contrastive fine-tuning (SimCSE or SupCon loss)
that explicitly pushes embeddings for similar-looking codes apart in
representation space. This is a research task estimated at 1-2 weeks of
implementation effort with uncertain but potentially large payoff.

---

## Context Window Constraint {#sec-limits-context}

Bio_ClinicalBERT's 512-token context window limits the amount of text the
model can see. Notes longer than ~400 words are silently truncated even
with APSO-Flip applied. The development dataset contains notes up to 1,300
words in length.

During evaluation, 15.6% of test records trigger the long-note warning
(>400 words). For these records, the model may be missing information
that would improve its prediction.

**Mitigation options:**
- **Longformer or Clinical-Longformer** — models with longer context windows
  (up to 4,096 tokens) at the cost of increased training time and memory.
- **Hierarchical encoding** — encode overlapping chunks separately and
  aggregate representations.
- **Better APSO ordering** — ensure that all critical information appears
  in the first 400 words through more aggressive section prioritisation.

---

## Single-Label Assumption {#sec-limits-single}

The system predicts a single primary ICD-10 code per note. Real clinical
coding typically assigns multiple codes per encounter — a primary diagnosis
plus secondary diagnoses, complications, and relevant comorbidities.

Extending to multi-label prediction requires a substantially different
output layer and evaluation methodology, and would require a multi-label
training dataset. This is outside the current scope.

---

## Hardware Portability {#sec-limits-hardware}

All development was conducted on Apple Silicon (M5 Max with MPS
acceleration). While the code is designed to be hardware-agnostic (with
CUDA fallback when MPS is not available), CUDA compatibility has not been
tested. The fp16 and dataloader_pin_memory settings are explicitly
configured for MPS compatibility and may need adjustment for CUDA environments.

---

## Future Work {#sec-future}

In priority order:

| Priority | Work Item | Expected Impact | Effort |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MIMIC-IV-Note validation | Quantifies synthetic-real gap | Medium (blocked on access) |
| 2 | Z-chapter contrastive fine-tuning | +5-10pp Z accuracy | Medium (~2 weeks) |
| 3 | Lower confidence threshold for Z | More Z cases auto-coded | Low |
| 4 | Multi-label prediction | Real-world applicability | High |
| 5 | Longer context models | Better long-note handling | High |
| 6 | Real-world dataset training | Production readiness | Very high |

: Future work priorities. {#tbl-future}
